header.stance: Oil-led Treasury relief broadened across real yields, with restrictive-rate risks intact
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 11 close overturned the prior session’s bear-steepening expression without settling the restrictive-policy debate beneath it. Treasuries rallied from the two-year sector through the long bond, and the 10-year yield finished at 4.45%, down 10 basis points. That outcome marked a decisive change from June 10, when softer core consumer inflation had failed to pull yields lower. The new catalyst was geopolitical: renewed confidence that the US-Iran conflict could move toward an agreement drove crude lower and removed part of the energy premium embedded in nominal yields and the expected Federal Reserve path.

The strongest transmission ran from de-escalation headlines through oil, inflation compensation and rate expectations. Short-rate contracts shifted a fully priced quarter-point increase from December into the first quarter of 2027. Real yields also fell, showing that the rally was broader than a mechanical drop in breakevens. Lower energy risk eased the prospective policy constraint, improved the growth-inflation mix and encouraged duration demand even as equities surged. This was therefore an oil-led repricing of the shock distribution, not a defensive flight from growth.

Domestic data supplied the main counterforce. May final-demand producer prices rose 1.1% from April, with energy lifting goods costs and underlying measures also exceeding expectations. Initial and continuing jobless claims moved higher, however, preventing the inflation surprise from becoming an unqualified tightening signal. The combination left the Fed with a difficult asymmetry: pipeline inflation remained too strong to validate easing, but softer labor indicators and a lower energy risk premium reduced the urgency of an increase.

Supply and global policy kept the rally from becoming a clean return to low-rate assumptions. The long-bond reopening was absorbed with improved cover and a large direct bid, yet the long end still lagged the belly slightly. The ECB raised rates because energy inflation was broadening, and officials left open another near-term increase. Credit markets reinforced the same conditional message: high-grade banks and large AI borrowers accessed substantial funding, but governance-impaired issuance failed even at a high yield. The governing interpretation changed from inflationary steepening to broad geopolitical relief, while elevated real yields, selective credit access and foreign tightening preserved a high hurdle for declaring the restrictive regime resolved.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

The Treasury rally was broad, with the belly outperforming at the margin. The two-year yield fell eight basis points to 4.05%, the five-year dropped nine to 4.18%, and the 10-year declined 10 to 4.45%. The 30-year yield ended eight basis points lower at 4.95%. The move returned the long bond below 5% and reversed the entire June 10 selloff, but policy-sensitive yields remained well above the federal funds target range. The close repriced the timing of possible restraint rather than introducing an easing path.

Curve changes were controlled. The 2s10s slope narrowed two basis points to 40, while 2s30s held at 90 because the two- and 30-year yields fell equally. The 2s5s10s butterfly was unchanged at minus 14, leaving the five-year sector’s relative shape stable. The 2s10s30s butterfly declined four basis points to minus 10 as the 10-year led the rally. This mild bull flattening contrasts with the prior day’s bear steepening, yet it did not create a new belly dislocation or an isolated long-end break.

Real yields and inflation compensation divided the nominal move evenly at 10 years. The 10-year TIPS yield fell five basis points to 2.16%, and the 10-year breakeven declined five to 2.29%. Five-year real yields also fell five basis points to 1.78%; seven-year yields dropped six to 1.96%, and 20- and 30-year real yields declined five and six to 2.53% and 2.72%. Falling breakevens captured the oil channel, while the parallel retreat in real yields reflected a lower perceived need for near-term policy tightening and less compensation for an adverse growth-inflation mix.

The longer comparison keeps the relief in perspective. Two-year yields were unchanged over one week and remained 10 basis points higher over one month. Five-year yields were also flat on the week and 11 basis points higher on the month. The 10-year was two basis points lower over one week but still three higher over one month, whereas the 30-year was two lower on the week and three lower on the month. The accumulated repricing remained concentrated in the front and belly, where policy timing and the expected neutral rate carry the most weight.

May producer prices challenged the rally’s durability. Final demand increased 1.1% on the month and 6.5% from a year earlier, against a 0.7% monthly public-calendar estimate. Final demand excluding food, energy and trade services rose 0.8% monthly and 5.1% annually, also above the 0.5% monthly estimate. Goods prices jumped 2.8%, led by a 10.7% increase in energy, while services rose 0.3%. The breadth beyond headline energy prevented the data from being dismissed as a single-component shock.

Labor data pointed the other way. Initial claims rose by 4,000 to 229,000 in the week ended June 6, above the 220,000 estimate, and the four-week average increased to 219,000. Continuing claims climbed by 24,000 to 1.795 million in the week ended May 30. The insured unemployment rate held at 1.2%, so the release did not signal a sudden labor rupture. It did show more workers entering and remaining on benefits, supplying a timely counterweight to the wholesale-inflation surprise.

Short-rate contracts resolved that conflict by delaying, rather than deleting, the tightening risk. Before the geopolitical headline, markets had fully priced a quarter-point increase by December. By the close, that timing had shifted into the first quarter of 2027. The reaction aligned with the joint fall in real yields and breakevens: cheaper oil reduced the risk of persistent pass-through, and firmer claims lowered the cost of waiting. Producer-price breadth still left the June 16-17 FOMC meeting unable to endorse a simple all-clear on inflation.

The $22 billion 30-year reopening cleared at 5.020% with a 2.33 bid-to-cover ratio, compared with 2.30 at the prior, larger sale. Indirect bidders took 59.95%, direct bidders 25.31% and dealers 14.74%. The 5% coupon bond settles June 15 and matures in May 2056. No when-issued benchmark was retained, so the cover and allocation mix define the demand signal. Improved cover, an unusually large direct share and a modest dealer residual showed end-user capacity, although the smaller offering reduced the balance-sheet burden relative to May.

Bill auctions showed similarly orderly but differentiated demand. Treasury accepted $70 billion of four-week bills at a 3.595% high rate with 3.13 cover, up from 3.07 previously; indirect bidders took 70.59%. The $75.001 billion eight-week sale cleared at 3.610% with 2.94 cover, down from 3.20, and a 69.59% indirect share. Both settle June 16. The shorter sale improved on a reduced amount, whereas unchanged eight-week supply drew softer cover. Neither result coincided with broad pressure in very-short cash yields.

Coupon-equivalent bill yields ended at 3.67% for four weeks and 3.68% for six weeks, both unchanged. The eight-week yield rose one basis point to 3.69%, while 13-, 17- and 26-week yields each fell one basis point to 3.71%, 3.76% and 3.80%. The 52-week yield declined five basis points to 3.85%, matching the delayed-hike adjustment in short-rate contracts. The front end remained upward sloping, but the policy premium migrated away from the immediate horizon.

Reserve management reduced the risk that the next increase in bill supply would become an abrupt funding shock. The Fed kept planned Treasury-bill purchases near $10 billion for the monthly period ending July 13 and scheduled about $16.5 billion of reinvestment purchases. Bank reserves had risen by $65.8 billion to $3.111 trillion in the week through June 10. Those operations are aimed at maintaining ample reserves as Treasury eventually rebuilds its cash balance above $1 trillion, a process that would otherwise withdraw liquidity from the banking system.

The rates close consequently carried two messages at once. Geopolitical relief was powerful enough to overpower a hot PPI release and pull both real yields and inflation compensation lower. Auction participation and reserve-management purchases kept supply from interrupting the move. Yet elevated producer-price breadth, an upward-sloping bill curve and still-high real yields prevented the rally from becoming an easing narrative. The next policy step looked later, not necessarily lower.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit participated in the broad improvement, with listed total-return gauges gaining alongside Treasuries and equities. LQD rose 0.8506%, HYG added 0.5914% and JNK gained 0.5745%. The larger investment-grade advance carried a meaningful duration contribution from falling government yields, while the high-yield gains showed that the session did not depend on a deterioration in corporate risk appetite. Primary markets remained open across ratings, although issuer structure and funding clarity produced sharply different outcomes.

Citibank demonstrated strong high-grade absorption with a $6.25 billion four-part sale spanning two to six years. Price talk on the longest tranche tightened to 67 basis points over Treasuries from roughly 95 initially. The operating-bank notes were expected to carry Aa3, A+ and AA- ratings from Moody’s, S&P and Fitch, and proceeds were designated for general corporate purposes. Citi had issued less than peers after capital-rule changes reduced its urgency, so the transaction added supply without looking like distressed balance-sheet repair.

The bank deal sat inside an active investment-grade calendar. The other five largest Wall Street banks had already sold more than $120 billion during the year, including record first-quarter issuance above $65 billion. Citi was one of three borrowers that brought more than $10 billion of US high-grade supply during the session. Tightening from initial talk showed that investors could absorb the late entrant despite the macro volatility, and the short-to-intermediate maturity range avoided placing the full burden on long-duration portfolios.

Oracle supplied a more specific cash-credit response to funding transparency. Its 3.65% note due 2041 tightened 15 basis points to a two-percentage-point spread by midday, and the 6.9% bond due 2052 narrowed 12 basis points to 2.09 percentage points. Five-year default protection also became cheaper. The improvement followed guidance that Oracle did not expect another calendar-year bond sale and would fund its current fiscal year with a planned $40 billion mix of debt and equity, including $20 billion of previously announced at-the-market stock issuance.

That relief did not remove the scale of Oracle’s capital needs. Fiscal-year capital expenditure reached $55.7 billion, above the company’s $50 billion projection, and net capital spending in the new year was expected near $70 billion. Customer prepayments and customer-supplied computing equipment can reduce Oracle’s immediate cash burden, but the company remains the largest nonfinancial issuer in the US high-grade index with about $117 billion of bonds. Credit investors rewarded a bounded financing plan even as the equity market questioned the returns on the spending itself.

CoreWeave extended AI funding into riskier and more global channels. The junk-rated cloud company sold €2 billion of six-year euro notes at an 8.5% yield and $1.25 billion of six-year dollar notes at 9.625%. Euro orders exceeded €7 billion and drew most major European high-yield buyers. Ratings of Ba3, B+ and BB- left the transaction far below hyperscaler quality, while projected investment near $35 billion and cash burn near $26 billion kept refinancing and execution risk high. The orderbook showed access; the yield preserved a large premium for leverage and uncertain profitability.

Hanuman Wind Power marked the boundary of that access. The Energy Absolute unit postponed its international junk-bond debut for a second time after extending order taking by a day. A mid-to-high-9% yield did not overcome concerns about the Thai parent’s governance, past fraud allegations and the intended transfer of some proceeds to the parent. Rising 15-year Treasury rates also weakened the relative valuation of the proposed 15-year non-call-five structure. Investors accepted aggressive AI expansion at a price, but rejected a structure whose governance and cash-flow perimeter remained unclear.

Primary absorption was therefore strong without becoming indiscriminate. High-grade bank debt tightened materially from initial talk, Oracle’s outstanding bonds rallied on a capped funding schedule, and CoreWeave diversified across currencies with a heavily subscribed euro tranche. Hanuman’s repeated postponement showed that yield alone could not repair weak governance. Falling Treasury yields improved the mechanical funding backdrop, yet disclosure, security position, buyer-base breadth and confidence in future capital needs determined how much of that benefit reached each borrower.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

The ECB moved against the direction of the US geopolitical repricing. It raised the deposit rate by 25 basis points to 2.25%, its first increase in almost three years, because the energy shock was broadening into indirect costs and inflation expectations. President Christine Lagarde described a slowdown in activity, especially services, but judged the economy able to absorb tighter policy. The decision made the euro area the first major economy to respond to the war-driven price shock with a hike.

European duration rallied despite the action. The 10-year yield fell three basis points to 3.05% after the announcement, and the euro held near $1.1538. That reaction suggests the hike was well anticipated and that the growth cost limited the market’s willingness to extrapolate an unlimited cycle. The ECB’s projections still kept inflation above target into the first half of 2027, leaving officials concerned that a peace agreement alone might not reverse second-round effects already moving through wages, services and expectations.

Follow-up risk remained meaningful. Officials did not exclude another increase as soon as July, although September remained the more common expectation, and investors priced two additional moves beyond the new 2.25% rate. The IMF assumed 50 basis points of cumulative tightening during 2026 and lifted its euro-area inflation forecast to 2.8% from 2.4%. It also reduced the growth outlook to 0.9% from 1.1%, defining the central bank’s constraint: broader inflation argues for restraint, but weaker demand can eventually reduce how much restraint is necessary.

Norway showed why the same energy shock need not produce synchronized action. A Norges Bank survey projected output growth of 0.2% in the second quarter and 0.3% in the third, below the prior 0.4% second-quarter view. Recruitment difficulty and capacity constraints eased, reducing pressure for another June hike despite a recent acceleration in core inflation. Wage-growth expectations rose to 4.5%, and the oil windfall supported the krone, so the case for holding rested on softer activity and a stronger currency rather than an absence of price pressure.

UK fiscal uncertainty added a separate long-duration premium ahead of the June 18 Makerfield by-election and Bank of England decision. Conventional gilts total about £2.1 trillion, while linkers and bills take public debt securities to roughly £2.9 trillion. National debt is projected at 105.4% of GDP in 2027, up from 98.8% in 2023. Investors remained wary of the long end because a possible Labour leadership challenge could alter borrowing plans, and the April budget deficit had already reached a six-year high for that month.

These developments pulled Treasury duration in opposing directions. Expected ECB follow-through and unsettled UK fiscal policy raise the return available on competing sovereign curves and can make global buyers demand more from long US bonds. Softer Norwegian activity and the rally in European government debt show that growth constraints still attract duration. June 11’s US rally overcame those external pressures because oil fell sharply, but it did not eliminate the buyer-base competition or fiscal uncertainty that supports elevated real term yields.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market debt benefited from the softer common backdrop. EMB gained 0.9649% as Treasury yields and oil fell, and the dollar weakened slightly rather than tightening global liquidity. Lower crude reduced the prospective import bill for fuel-dependent economies and delayed the expected Fed hike, easing both base-rate and currency pressure. The improvement was broad enough to lift the listed hard-currency gauge, but local sovereign performance remained dominated by policy credibility and refinancing structure.

Indonesia remained the clearest adverse case. The benchmark 10-year local yield rose 10 basis points to 7.45%, near its highest since 2022, while the rupiah fell 0.2% and remained about 7% weaker for the year. Foreign investors had withdrawn a net $597 million from bonds and $3.9 billion from equities. Bank Indonesia had signaled that it would tolerate higher yields to support the currency, and analysts saw scope for another 25 to 50 basis points of tightening. Currency defense was therefore raising the sovereign’s domestic funding cost even as global conditions improved.

Danantara’s debut dollar bond showed that external access was available at a price. Indonesia’s sovereign wealth fund raised $1.5 billion through equal five- and 10-year tranches. The five-year notes yielded 5.35%, and both tranches priced about 35 basis points tighter than initial talk. Final books were less than twice covered, below the three-times-or-better demand common in many recent Asian deals. The transaction gave a state-linked borrower market access, but the modest oversubscription and recent widening in Indonesian dollar spreads preserved differentiation from the sovereign itself.

The contrast between Danantara’s execution and the local selloff captures Indonesia’s policy problem. Dollar investors accepted a defined security and maturity package, whereas local bonds still had to absorb currency defense, oil-import costs and concerns about interventionist policy. The fund was also considering local-currency bonds with below-market coupons, a proposal that could test domestic investors’ willingness to support strategic state financing. Successful offshore issuance therefore eased one funding need without resolving the confidence premium embedded in the rupiah curve.

Zimbabwe remained a debt-restructuring case rather than a market-access story. The government was discussing a $150 million African Development Bank loan as part of a broader effort to raise $2.5 billion for arrears clearance. It has been excluded from international capital markets since 1999 and is seeking to restructure about $23 billion of debt. An IMF staff-monitored program and an earlier $4 million AfDB grant improve the institutional path, but bridge financing must still convert into creditor normalization before conventional sovereign issuance becomes realistic.

Peru provided a more stable policy counterexample. The central bank held its benchmark rate at 4.25% for a ninth month as Lima inflation eased to 3.91% from 4.01%. Inflation remained above the 1%-3% target range after energy, gas and food shocks, yet the first monthly moderation since October reduced the case for an immediate increase. A razor-thin presidential vote count kept political risk active, but the rate decision showed that credible institutions could wait for temporary supply pressure to dissipate rather than tighten automatically.

The EM close was thus a relief rally around persistent country dispersion. Falling oil and a softer dollar improved the shared financing environment, and Danantara demonstrated access to external capital. Indonesia’s local curve still demanded a policy and confidence premium, Zimbabwe remained dependent on arrears clearance, and Peru could hold only because inflation had begun to ease. Sovereign, quasi-sovereign and corporate channels responded differently to the same global move because their currency exposure, institutional credibility and refinancing options were not interchangeable.

## Cross asset

Oil supplied the clearest cross-asset confirmation of the Treasury rally. WTI futures fell 2.5769% to $87.71 and Brent dropped 2.9216% to $90.38 after the US president canceled planned strikes and said an agreement with Iran could be announced. Both benchmarks remained high enough to sustain concern about pipeline inflation, but the daily retreat removed part of the war premium. The simultaneous declines in crude and breakevens aligned the market move with de-escalation rather than a generic demand shock.

Equities reinforced that interpretation. The S&P 500 rose 1.7519%, the Nasdaq 100 gained 3.2908% and the semiconductor index jumped 7.9055%. The VIX fell 12.5112% to 19.44. A growth scare severe enough to generate a 10-basis-point Treasury rally would normally struggle to coexist with such strong technology performance and collapsing volatility. Instead, lower energy risk improved the prospective inflation-policy mix while preserving nominal growth and appetite for long-duration earnings.

The dollar and gold also resisted a conventional haven configuration. The dollar index slipped 0.09% to 99.86, and gold futures fell 0.4357% to $4,090.30. Neither asset showed demand for protection against an imminent geopolitical escalation. The modest dollar decline helped emerging-market debt participate in the rally, while gold’s weakness matched lower war insurance and firmer risk appetite. These moves left real-yield relief, not a scramble for safety, as the cleaner explanation for the Treasury advance.

Cross assets nevertheless kept one question open. Oil fell on confidence in negotiations that had repeatedly shifted during the conflict, and producer-price breadth showed that earlier energy pressure had already entered parts of the supply chain. Equities priced a favorable resolution more aggressively than the inflation data alone would justify, while Treasury levels still embedded a restrictive real-rate regime. The alignment was powerful for one close, but its durability depends on realized de-escalation and a continued retreat in household and business inflation expectations.

## Forward Market Significance

The June University of Michigan preliminary survey on June 12 at 10:00 ET, followed by US industrial production on June 15 at 09:15, import and export prices plus housing starts on June 16 at 08:30, and retail sales on June 17 at 08:30, will test whether the energy shock is fading before the FOMC decision at 14:00 that day. The observable trigger is a renewed rise in household inflation expectations or broad trade-price pressure alongside resilient production and spending, versus softer expectations and weakening interest-sensitive activity. The former would challenge June 11’s lower-breakeven, delayed-hike interpretation and pressure two- to 10-year yields into Chair Kevin Warsh’s first policy communication. The latter would confirm that claims and cheaper oil are overtaking PPI as the marginal policy input, allowing the Fed to hold its 3.50%-3.75% range without signaling a near-term increase.

Treasury’s June 15-18 cash calendar will test whether orderly auction absorption survives concentrated settlement and a renewed supply sequence. The $119 billion gross settlement of three-, 10- and 30-year coupons arrives June 15, alongside $89 billion of 13-week and $77 billion of 26-week bill auctions; June 16 brings a $65 billion six-week bill sale and a $13 billion 20-year reopening, followed by a $24 billion five-year TIPS reopening on June 18. Observable triggers are cover, end-user and dealer shares, the 20-year stop against its then-current when-issued level, short-term funding rates and demand for real yield at the TIPS sale. Strong end-user absorption with stable funding would confirm that June 11’s rally was macro-led and resolve part of the balance-sheet concern. Heavy dealer awards, weak duration demand or settlement strain would reopen supply and reserve scarcity as independent pressures on term premium.

The Bank of Japan and Reserve Bank of Australia decisions on June 16, followed by Bank Indonesia, the Swiss National Bank, Norges Bank, the Bank of England and the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas on June 18, will test whether lower oil produces policy divergence after the ECB’s hike. The observable triggers are whether the BOJ delivers the expected increase toward 1%, whether the RBA and European hold decisions retain tightening language, and whether Indonesia or the Philippines raises rates to defend currencies against imported inflation. Stable JGB and gilt long ends, firmer EM currencies and less hawkish guidance would challenge the view that foreign curves must keep lifting the Treasury clearing rate. Additional hikes, disorderly duration selling or renewed currency defense would confirm global competition for capital and reopen the imported-inflation channel that June 11’s oil decline temporarily relieved.

section_summaries.market_read: The close reset the dominant risk from inflationary steepening to conditional geopolitical relief.
section_summaries.next_check: Realized de-escalation must keep oil and inflation expectations retreating beyond one session.
section_summaries.summary: De-escalation hopes pulled crude lower and reversed the prior session's bear steepening, drawing nominal and real yields down as rate-increase timing moved later. Hot producer prices, foreign tightening and selective credit access still left the restrictive-policy regime unresolved.

section_summaries.market_read: The rally repriced the timing of restraint without opening a credible path toward policy easing.
section_summaries.next_check: Upcoming inflation, activity and FOMC signals must resolve the conflict between PPI and claims.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Broad Treasury gains produced a mild bull flattening as the 10-year sector led, while real yields and breakevens fell in equal measure. Delayed tightening, orderly auctions and ample reserves supported duration, but broad producer-price pressure kept the next policy step later rather than lower.

section_summaries.market_read: Credit confirmed broad risk appetite, but issuer quality still determined access and pricing.
section_summaries.next_check: New-deal books must show whether strong demand survives without unusually clear funding plans.
section_summaries.summary: Primary markets absorbed large bank and AI-related borrowing, while existing Oracle debt improved after its funding plan became more bounded. CoreWeave secured costly cross-currency access, but Hanuman's repeated postponement showed that falling Treasury yields could not overcome weak governance and unclear cash-flow boundaries.

section_summaries.market_read: Foreign policy and fiscal risks kept global term premiums from validating a low-rate regime.
section_summaries.next_check: June central-bank decisions must reveal whether lower oil reduces further tightening pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: The ECB tightened as energy inflation broadened, yet European duration rallied because the move was anticipated and weaker growth constrained follow-through. Softer Norwegian activity and UK fiscal uncertainty pulled global duration in opposite directions, preserving competition for long-end Treasury buyers.

section_summaries.market_read: Global relief supported hard-currency debt without erasing sovereign credibility premiums.
section_summaries.next_check: Currency moves and policy decisions must show whether EM relief survives renewed oil pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: Lower oil, a softer dollar and delayed Fed tightening improved the common EM backdrop, but country outcomes remained sharply differentiated. Indonesia's currency defense raised local funding costs despite offshore access, Zimbabwe still required arrears clearance, and Peru's easing inflation allowed patience.

section_summaries.market_read: Risk assets validated an improved inflation-policy mix, not a defensive rush into Treasuries.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil and household inflation expectations must keep falling for the cross-asset alignment to hold.
section_summaries.summary: Crude, breakevens and haven demand fell as equities surged and volatility retreated, confirming that the Treasury rally reflected de-escalation rather than a growth shock. The alignment remained conditional because earlier energy pressure had entered producer costs and negotiations could still reverse.

section_summaries.market_read: The rally remains contingent on macro disinflation, auction capacity and global policy divergence.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch the June 12–18 sequence for failures in breakevens, funding or foreign duration.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests will determine whether the close becomes durable: US data and the FOMC, concentrated Treasury supply, and coordinated global policy decisions. Softer inflation signals, orderly end-user absorption and less hawkish foreign guidance would support relief; opposite outcomes would revive policy, funding and term-premium pressure.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.16% (-5 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.29% (-5 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 40 bp (-2 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 90 bp (0 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasuries rallied as de-escalation cut oil risk, but inflation kept policy restrictive
overall_summary.session_narrative: At the completed NY close, Treasuries had rallied across maturities, with the belly leading a mild bull flattening. De-escalation headlines lowered crude's war premium, easing breakevens and delaying the fully priced quarter-point increase into early 2027; falling real yields showed broader relief. Strong producer-price breadth, elevated real yields and selective credit access limited any shift toward an easing narrative.
overall_summary.summary: Broad Treasury gains replaced the prior session's bear steepening as confidence in a US-Iran agreement pulled crude and inflation compensation lower. Real yields also declined, but hot producer prices, still-high real rates and foreign tightening kept the delayed-hike repricing conditional.

watch_events.market_implication: The first mix would pressure two- to 10-year yields; the second would validate a delayed hike.
watch_events.market_trigger: Rising expectations and trade prices with resilient demand, or softer interest-sensitive activity.
watch_events.title: US data and FOMC policy test
watch_events.watch_line: Track inflation expectations, trade prices, activity and spending into the FOMC decision.

watch_events.market_implication: Strain would restore supply and reserve scarcity as independent sources of term-premium pressure.
watch_events.market_trigger: Heavy dealer awards, a weak 20-year stop or strained funding would signal poor absorption.
watch_events.title: Treasury settlement and auction absorption
watch_events.watch_line: Watch cover, bidder mix, funding rates and 20-year and TIPS demand through settlement.

watch_events.market_implication: The first would ease Treasury clearing pressure; the second would reopen imported inflation.
watch_events.market_trigger: Stable long ends and firmer EM currencies, or added hikes and renewed currency defense.
watch_events.title: Global central-bank divergence test
watch_events.watch_line: Track BOJ, RBA and June 18 decisions for rates, guidance and long-end stability.
